architecture_patterns.name: Hub-and-Spoke Federation
architecture_patterns.description: Hub-and-Spoke Federation establishes a centralized Identity Provider (IdP) as the trust hub with multiple Service Providers (SPs) connected as spokes. All authentication flows through the central hub IdP, which maintains the authoritative user directory and issues security tokens accepted by all participating SPs. This is the dominant federation topology in enterprise environments using Azure AD (Entra ID), Okta, or PingFederate as the central IdP. The hub IdP handles all authentication protocols (SAML, OIDC, WS-Fed), MFA enforcement, conditional access policies, and single sign-on session management. SPs only need to trust the single hub IdP rather than establishing bilateral trust with every other SP, reducing the number of trust relationships from O(n^2) to O(n).
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- Enterprise environments with a single authoritative identity source (Active Directory, HR system)
- Organizations standardizing on a single IdP platform (Azure AD, Okta, PingFederate)
- When centralized authentication policy enforcement (MFA, conditional access) is required
- SaaS application portfolio where each app needs SSO with the corporate directory
- When reducing trust relationship complexity is a priority (n relationships instead of n^2)
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Not suitable for decentralized organizations where no single IdP has authority over all users (use mesh federation instead). Problematic for B2B scenarios where partner organizations will not redirect authentication to your hub IdP. Creates a single point of failure - if the hub IdP is unavailable, all SPs lose authentication capability. Avoid when regulatory requirements prohibit routing authentication through a third-party IdP.
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. User accesses an SP application (spoke). 2. SP redirects user to the hub IdP for authentication. 3. Hub IdP authenticates the user (password, MFA, certificate) and evaluates conditional access policies. 4. Hub IdP issues a security token (SAML assertion or OIDC ID token) with user claims. 5. User is redirected back to the SP with the token. 6. SP validates the token against the hub IdP's signing keys and grants access. 7. Subsequent SP access reuses the hub IdP's SSO session without re-authentication.
architecture_patterns.strengths: Simple topology that is easy to understand, implement, and audit. Centralized policy enforcement ensures consistent authentication requirements across all SPs. Single sign-on experience is seamless once authenticated at the hub. Reduced trust management - each SP only trusts the hub, not every other SP. Well-supported by all major IdP vendors with template SP integrations. Centralized audit trail of all authentication events.
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: Single point of failure - hub IdP outage blocks all SP access. Hub IdP vendor lock-in since all SPs depend on hub-specific token formats and claims. Scalability bottleneck under high authentication load (all auth flows through one point). Cross-organizational B2B scenarios require the external org to trust your hub. Protocol limitations when the hub must translate between SAML and OIDC for different SPs. Token translation at the hub can lose claims or introduce security weaknesses.

architecture_patterns.name: Mesh Federation
architecture_patterns.description: Mesh Federation establishes peer-to-peer trust relationships between multiple Identity Providers, where each IdP can authenticate users and assert their identity to any other IdP or SP in the mesh. Unlike hub-and-spoke, there is no single central authority - each participating organization maintains its own IdP and establishes bilateral or multilateral trust agreements with peers. This topology is used in academic federations (InCommon, eduGAIN), government inter-agency identity sharing (FICAM), and multi-national enterprise consortiums. Mesh federation requires federation metadata aggregation services that distribute trust information across all participants, enabling dynamic discovery of partner IdPs. The trade-off is increased trust management complexity (O(n^2) potential relationships) in exchange for no single point of failure and no central authority requirement.
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- Multi-organization consortiums where no single entity should be the central trust authority
- Academic and research federations (InCommon, eduGAIN) with peer-based governance
- Government inter-agency identity sharing where each agency maintains its own IdP
- B2B ecosystems where partner organizations authenticate their own users
- Disaster-resilient architectures where single IdP failure must not block all access
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Avoid for single-organization environments where hub-and-spoke is simpler and sufficient. The trust management complexity of O(n^2) relationships makes mesh impractical for large numbers of participants without a metadata aggregation service. Not suitable when centralized policy enforcement is required (each IdP sets its own policies). Difficult to ensure consistent authentication strength across autonomous IdPs in the mesh.
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. User accesses a resource at Organization B's SP. 2. SP presents an IdP discovery page (or uses home realm discovery) for the user to select their home IdP. 3. User is redirected to their home IdP (Organization A). 4. Home IdP authenticates the user and issues a token with the SP's required attributes. 5. SP validates the token using the home IdP's metadata (keys, endpoints) obtained from the federation metadata service. 6. SP maps the received attributes to local authorization policies. 7. Each IdP publishes its metadata to the aggregation service for discovery by all mesh participants.
architecture_patterns.strengths: No single point of failure or central authority dependency. Each organization retains full sovereignty over its user identities and authentication policies. Scales to large inter-organizational federations (InCommon has 1,000+ members). Bilateral trust is more resilient than hub dependency. Well-established governance models from academic federation experience. Supports diverse authentication methods across participating organizations.
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: Trust relationship complexity grows quadratically with participants. Inconsistent authentication strength across IdPs (one IdP might allow passwords-only while another requires phishing-resistant MFA). Attribute mapping between organizations is error-prone and requires ongoing governance. Metadata management and key rotation across many participants is operationally complex. IdP discovery UX can confuse users who must select their home organization. Harder to audit than hub-and-spoke due to distributed nature.

architecture_patterns.name: Identity Broker
architecture_patterns.description: An Identity Broker acts as an intermediary that translates between different identity protocols and providers, shielding Service Providers from the complexity of supporting multiple IdPs directly. The broker accepts authentication requests from SPs, routes users to their appropriate upstream IdP (enterprise AD, social providers, partner federations), translates the resulting tokens into the SP's required format, and normalizes attribute names and values. Implementations include Keycloak, Azure AD B2C, Auth0, ForgeRock Access Management, and WSO2 Identity Server. The broker pattern is essential for B2C and B2B2C scenarios where a single application must accept identities from hundreds of sources (social logins, enterprise SSO, government eIDs). The broker provides a single integration point for SPs while handling the heterogeneity of upstream identity sources.
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- Applications needing to accept identities from multiple heterogeneous IdPs (enterprise, social, government)
- B2C platforms where customers authenticate via Google, Apple, Facebook, and enterprise SSO
- Protocol translation scenarios: upstream IdPs use SAML but SPs require OIDC (or vice versa)
- Mergers and acquisitions where multiple legacy identity systems must be unified
- When SPs should not need to know which upstream IdP authenticated the user
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Unnecessary when all users authenticate through a single IdP (use direct federation instead). Adds latency and an additional point of failure in the authentication path. Avoid when the broker becomes a 'god service' that concentrates all identity risk. Not suitable when end-to-end token integrity from IdP to SP is required (broker re-signs tokens, breaking the chain). Problematic for high-security scenarios where introducing an intermediary increases the attack surface.
components: Upstream IdP Connectors (SAML, OIDC, LDAP, Social)
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. User accesses SP application, which redirects to the identity broker. 2. Broker presents IdP discovery (enterprise SSO, social login options). 3. User selects their IdP; broker redirects to the upstream IdP. 4. Upstream IdP authenticates the user and returns a token to the broker. 5. Broker validates the upstream token, extracts attributes, applies normalization rules. 6. Broker issues a new token in the SP's required format (e.g., translates SAML to OIDC). 7. SP receives the broker-issued token and authorizes based on normalized claims. 8. Broker optionally performs JIT provisioning to create/update a local user account.
architecture_patterns.strengths: Decouples SPs from upstream IdP complexity - SPs integrate once with the broker. Enables protocol translation between heterogeneous identity systems. Supports diverse authentication sources through a unified interface. JIT provisioning simplifies user onboarding from external IdPs. Centralized attribute normalization ensures consistent claim formats across all sources. Supports progressive identity enrichment (step-up auth, additional attribute collection).
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: Single point of failure and high-value attack target (compromising the broker compromises all SPs). Additional authentication latency from the extra redirect hop. Token re-issuance breaks end-to-end token integrity from the original IdP. Complex operational management of multiple upstream IdP connections. Broker must be kept patched and hardened as it handles all authentication traffic. Vendor lock-in to the broker platform (migrating between Keycloak, Auth0, Azure B2C is non-trivial).

architecture_patterns.name: SSO Gateway / Reverse Proxy SSO
architecture_patterns.description: An SSO Gateway is a reverse proxy that sits in front of applications and handles authentication on their behalf, injecting identity information into upstream requests via HTTP headers, cookies, or tokens. This pattern enables SSO for legacy applications that cannot be modified to support modern federation protocols (SAML, OIDC). The gateway intercepts all requests, checks for a valid session, redirects unauthenticated users to the IdP, and upon successful authentication, forwards requests to the backend application with identity headers (e.g., X-Remote-User, X-Remote-Groups). Implementations include Azure AD Application Proxy, Okta Access Gateway, PingAccess, SiteMinder (now Symantec SSO), and open-source solutions like OAuth2 Proxy and Pomerium. The pattern is critical for organizations with legacy applications that cannot be refactored for modern authentication.
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- Legacy applications that cannot implement SAML/OIDC natively (mainframe UIs, COTS products, internal tools)
- Providing SSO for applications that only support header-based authentication
- Centralizing authentication and pre-authentication for a portfolio of web applications
- Exposing internal applications externally with identity-aware access control (zero trust replacement for VPN)
- When application modernization is not feasible but modern authentication is required
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Avoid for modern applications that can natively integrate OIDC/SAML - direct federation is more secure and performant. Not suitable for non-HTTP protocols (SSH, RDP, database connections) without protocol-specific tunnel support. The header injection pattern is vulnerable to header spoofing if backend applications are directly accessible bypassing the gateway. Avoid when application-level authorization requires token-based claims that cannot be expressed as HTTP headers.
components: Reverse Proxy / Gateway (nginx, Envoy, Azure App Proxy)
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. User requests application URL; request hits the SSO gateway. 2. Gateway checks for valid session cookie. 3. If no session, gateway redirects to IdP (Azure AD, Okta) for authentication. 4. IdP authenticates user and returns token to gateway callback. 5. Gateway creates a session, stores identity data, and sets a session cookie. 6. Gateway forwards the original request to the backend with injected identity headers (X-Remote-User, X-Remote-Email, X-Remote-Groups). 7. Backend application reads headers and authorizes the user. 8. Subsequent requests use the session cookie; gateway re-injects headers on each request.
architecture_patterns.strengths: Enables SSO for legacy applications without any modification to application code. Centralizes authentication and access policy enforcement at the network layer. Provides pre-authentication that prevents unauthenticated traffic from reaching backend applications. Can enforce MFA and conditional access for applications that do not natively support them. Simplifies zero trust network access by providing identity-aware proxying. Reduces attack surface by shielding backend applications from direct internet exposure.
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: Header spoofing vulnerability if backend applications are accessible without going through the gateway. Single point of failure for all proxied applications. Performance overhead from proxying all traffic through the gateway. Session management complexity when applications have their own sessions alongside gateway sessions. Limited to HTTP(S) traffic without additional protocol support. Backend applications trust headers blindly, creating risk if gateway is misconfigured or bypassed.

architecture_patterns.name: SAML-Based Federation
architecture_patterns.description: SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) federation enables cross-domain single sign-on using XML-based security assertions exchanged between an Identity Provider (IdP) and Service Provider (SP). SAML 2.0, standardized by OASIS in 2005, defines three core assertions: Authentication (the user proved their identity), Attribute (claims about the user), and Authorization Decision (access grant). The protocol uses browser redirects with signed XML documents to enable SSO without the SP needing direct access to the IdP's user store. SAML remains the dominant enterprise federation protocol for SaaS applications, with support from Salesforce, ServiceNow, AWS, and virtually every enterprise SaaS product. While OIDC is increasingly preferred for new implementations, SAML's installed base in enterprise environments means it will remain relevant for many years.
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- Enterprise SaaS application integration where the SaaS vendor requires SAML SSO
- Environments with existing SAML infrastructure and established trust relationships
- Government and regulated industries where SAML is mandated or has established compliance evidence
- Cross-domain SSO scenarios where both parties already support SAML 2.0
- Applications requiring signed and optionally encrypted assertions for compliance
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Avoid for new mobile or SPA applications where OIDC/OAuth 2.0 is a better fit (SAML's XML handling is problematic in mobile/JavaScript contexts). Not suitable for API-to-API authorization (use OAuth 2.0 instead). SAML's complexity and XML parsing vulnerabilities (XXE, XML signature wrapping) make it higher risk than OIDC for new implementations. Avoid when the receiving application needs fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes rather than SAML's binary assertion model.
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. User accesses SP application (SP-initiated SSO). 2. SP generates AuthnRequest, signs it, and redirects user to IdP SSO endpoint. 3. IdP authenticates user (if not already authenticated). 4. IdP creates SAML Response containing signed Assertion with authentication statement, attributes, and conditions. 5. Response is POSTed to SP's Assertion Consumer Service (ACS) URL via the user's browser. 6. SP validates XML signature, checks assertion conditions (NotBefore, NotOnOrAfter, Audience), and extracts attributes. 7. SP creates a local session and grants access based on assertion attributes.
architecture_patterns.strengths: Mature, well-understood protocol with 20 years of enterprise deployment experience. Supported by virtually every enterprise SaaS application. XML signatures provide strong assertion integrity and optional confidentiality (encryption). Rich assertion model supports complex attribute statements. Established compliance evidence for regulatory frameworks. Federation metadata enables automated trust configuration.
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: XML parsing vulnerabilities (XXE, XML Signature Wrapping attacks) require careful implementation. Verbose XML format is poorly suited for mobile and SPA applications. No native support for API authorization (not a replacement for OAuth 2.0). Complex specification with many optional features leading to interoperability issues. Single Logout (SLO) is unreliable across implementations. Session management between IdP and SP sessions can create security gaps. Being superseded by OIDC for new implementations.

architecture_patterns.name: OIDC-Based Federation
architecture_patterns.description: OpenID Connect (OIDC) federation enables cross-domain single sign-on and identity assertion using the OAuth 2.0 protocol extended with an identity layer. The IdP (called OpenID Provider or OP) authenticates users and issues ID Tokens (signed JWTs containing identity claims) along with Access Tokens for API authorization. OIDC 1.0, standardized by the OpenID Foundation in 2014, defines three authentication flows: Authorization Code (for server-side apps), Implicit (deprecated), and Hybrid. OIDC is the modern replacement for SAML in most scenarios, offering simpler JSON-based tokens, native mobile/SPA support, built-in API authorization via OAuth 2.0 scopes, and dynamic client registration. All major IdPs (Azure AD, Google, Okta, Auth0, Keycloak) support OIDC as their primary federation protocol.
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- New application development requiring SSO and API authorization
- Mobile and single-page applications (SPAs) where SAML's XML handling is impractical
- API-first architectures needing both identity federation and resource authorization
- Consumer-facing applications requiring social login integration
- Microservices requiring JWT-based inter-service authentication and authorization
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Avoid when integrating with legacy enterprise SaaS that only supports SAML 2.0 (many older enterprise apps do not support OIDC). Not suitable when assertion encryption is required and the SP cannot handle JWE (JWT Encryption). Some government and regulated environments still mandate SAML for compliance evidence. Avoid the Implicit flow entirely (deprecated in OAuth 2.1) - use Authorization Code with PKCE instead.
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. User accesses RP application, which redirects to OP's authorization endpoint with scope=openid. 2. OP authenticates user and obtains consent for requested scopes. 3. OP redirects back with authorization code (Authorization Code flow with PKCE). 4. RP exchanges authorization code for ID Token + Access Token at token endpoint. 5. RP validates ID Token signature (via JWKS), checks issuer, audience, nonce, and expiry. 6. RP extracts claims from ID Token (sub, email, name, custom claims). 7. RP optionally calls UserInfo endpoint with Access Token for additional claims. 8. RP creates local session and authorizes based on token claims.
architecture_patterns.strengths: JSON-based tokens (JWT) are compact, easy to parse, and work well in mobile/SPA contexts. Built-in API authorization through OAuth 2.0 scopes and Access Tokens. Dynamic discovery (.well-known/openid-configuration) simplifies configuration. PKCE flow provides security for public clients without client secrets. Simpler than SAML to implement, with fewer parsing vulnerabilities. Universal IdP support and growing SaaS adoption. Active standards development (OIDC Federation, DPoP, RAR).
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: JWT tokens are base64-encoded, not encrypted by default (use JWE when confidentiality is needed). Token revocation before expiry is not natively supported (requires introspection or short-lived tokens). Less mature compliance evidence than SAML in some regulated industries. Front-channel logout is less reliable than SAML SLO. Token bloat when many claims are included (HTTP header limits). Multiple flows and extensions create implementation confusion (which flow to use when).

architecture_patterns.name: Business-to-Business (B2B) Federation
architecture_patterns.description: B2B Federation enables secure identity sharing between separate organizations, allowing employees of a partner organization to access your applications using their home organization's credentials. Instead of creating guest accounts with separate passwords, B2B federation establishes a trust relationship between the partner's IdP and your SP, so partner users authenticate at their own IdP and present a trusted assertion to your application. Azure AD B2B Collaboration, Okta Org2Org, and SAML-based bilateral federation are common implementations. B2B federation eliminates the need to manage external user credentials, reduces account lifecycle risks (when a partner employee leaves, their home IdP revokes access automatically), and provides a better user experience than guest accounts. Key challenges include attribute mapping between organizations, assurance level mismatches, and trust governance.
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- Partner organizations need access to your applications without creating local guest accounts
- Supply chain and vendor collaboration requiring authenticated access to shared resources
- Joint ventures and consortium environments where multiple companies share platforms
- When you need automated deprovisioning when a partner employee leaves their organization
- Regulated industries requiring traceable authentication back to the partner's identity system
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Not suitable when the partner organization has no IdP capable of federation (small vendors may only have email accounts). Avoid when you need full control over the authentication strength and method used by external users (you must trust the partner's IdP policies). Problematic when partner IdPs are unreliable - their outage blocks their users from accessing your resources. Not appropriate for consumer-facing scenarios (use social login or CIAM instead).
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. Partner user is invited or accesses your application directly. 2. Home realm discovery identifies the user's home organization (by email domain or explicit selection). 3. User is redirected to their organization's IdP for authentication. 4. Partner IdP authenticates the user using their corporate credentials and MFA policies. 5. Partner IdP issues a token asserting the user's identity and attributes. 6. Your application (or identity broker) validates the partner token, maps attributes to local roles. 7. Application creates or updates a guest account representation (shadow account) for authorization. 8. When partner revokes the user at their IdP, the next authentication attempt fails, effectively deprovisioning access.
architecture_patterns.strengths: Eliminates external credential management - partner controls their own user lifecycle. Automatic deprovisioning when partner employees leave (no stale guest accounts). Better user experience - partners use their familiar corporate credentials. Audit trail traces back to partner's authentication event. Scales to many partners without proportional increase in user management overhead. Reduces help desk burden for password resets and account lockouts.
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: Dependency on partner IdP availability - their outage affects your users. Assurance level uncertainty - you cannot control the partner's MFA or password policies. Attribute mapping between organizations is manual and error-prone. Trust governance requires ongoing relationship management and periodic review. Shadow account proliferation if many partners federate with your organization. Inconsistent user experience when different partners have different IdP capabilities and authentication flows.

architecture_patterns.name: Social Login Federation
architecture_patterns.description: Social Login Federation allows consumer-facing applications to accept authentication from social identity providers such as Google, Apple, Facebook/Meta, Microsoft, GitHub, and LinkedIn. Instead of requiring users to create a new account with a password, the application delegates authentication to a social provider the user already trusts, reducing registration friction and password fatigue. Social login is implemented via OAuth 2.0/OIDC with each social provider acting as an OpenID Provider. The application receives an ID token or profile API response containing the user's verified email, name, and profile picture. Apple Sign In requires special handling (it only provides the user's name on first login). Social login is a cornerstone of Customer Identity and Access Management (CIAM) and is used by virtually all consumer-facing web and mobile applications to reduce registration abandonment rates (typically 25-40% lower than password-based registration).
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- Consumer-facing web and mobile applications prioritizing frictionless registration
- Applications where reducing registration abandonment is a business priority
- B2C platforms where users have no enterprise IdP (consumer market)
- Applications needing verified email addresses without implementing email verification flows
- Mobile applications where Apple Sign In is required by App Store policy
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Not appropriate for enterprise internal applications (use enterprise SSO instead). Avoid as the sole authentication method - always provide email+password or passwordless as fallback (social providers can revoke API access). Not suitable when the application needs strong identity verification (social identity is self-asserted, not identity-proofed). Problematic in privacy-sensitive contexts where users do not want to share data with social providers. Avoid for applications accessed in countries where certain social providers are blocked.
components: Account Linking Service (merge social and local accounts)
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. User clicks 'Sign in with Google/Apple/Facebook' button. 2. Application redirects to social provider's authorization endpoint with requested scopes. 3. User authenticates at the social provider (if not already signed in) and consents to share profile data. 4. Social provider redirects back with authorization code. 5. Application exchanges code for ID token and access token. 6. Application extracts profile claims (email, name, picture) from the ID token or profile API. 7. Application creates or links a local account using the social provider's unique subject identifier. 8. Local session is established; social token is stored for optional profile refresh.
architecture_patterns.strengths: Dramatically reduces registration friction (25-40% lower abandonment). Users do not need to create and remember another password. Verified email from social provider eliminates email verification flow. Familiar UX that users trust. Free identity verification leveraging social provider's existing anti-fraud systems. Fast implementation with well-documented SDKs and OIDC flows.
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: Dependency on social provider availability and API stability (Facebook has changed APIs multiple times). Privacy concerns about sharing user data with social providers (GDPR implications). Account recovery complexity when a user loses access to their social account. Account linking challenges when users sign up with social and later want to add a password (or vice versa). Social providers can revoke your API access, breaking authentication. Limited identity assurance - social identity is self-asserted, not identity-proofed. Different providers return different claim sets, requiring normalization.

architecture_patterns.name: Step-Up Authentication
architecture_patterns.description: Step-Up Authentication dynamically escalates the authentication strength required based on the sensitivity of the requested action or resource, the current risk assessment, or the time elapsed since the last strong authentication. A user who authenticated with a password can access low-sensitivity resources, but attempting to transfer funds, modify security settings, or access PII triggers a step-up challenge requiring a stronger factor (FIDO2, push notification, OTP). This pattern is codified in NIST SP 800-63B as authentication assurance level (AAL) progression and in OpenID Connect as the 'acr' (Authentication Context Class Reference) claim. Implementations use the OIDC 'acr_values' parameter, SAML AuthnContext, or custom risk engine signals to trigger step-up. The pattern balances security and usability by requiring strong authentication only when the risk justifies it.
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- Financial applications requiring stronger authentication for high-value transactions
- Healthcare systems requiring additional verification to access sensitive patient data
- Any application where certain actions are significantly more sensitive than baseline access
- When risk-adaptive authentication is needed to balance security with user friction
- Compliance with PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) for payment initiation
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Unnecessary for applications where all resources have the same sensitivity level and a single authentication strength suffices. Avoid when the UX disruption of mid-session authentication challenges would cause unacceptable user abandonment. Not suitable when the application cannot determine action sensitivity at request time (requires well-defined sensitivity classification). Problematic for API-only services where interactive step-up challenges are not feasible.
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. User authenticates with baseline credentials (password = AAL1) and accesses general resources. 2. User attempts a sensitive action (fund transfer, PII access, security settings change). 3. Application or API gateway evaluates the required authentication level for this action. 4. If the current session's AAL is below the required level, a step-up challenge is triggered. 5. User completes the stronger authentication factor (FIDO2 key, biometric, push approval). 6. Session's authentication level is upgraded; the timestamp of the strong authentication is recorded. 7. Sensitive action is authorized. 8. After a configurable timeout, the elevated authentication level expires and future sensitive actions require re-authentication.
architecture_patterns.strengths: Balances security and usability - strong auth only when risk warrants it. Aligned with NIST 800-63B AAL model and PSD2 SCA requirements. Reduces user friction for routine low-risk operations. Risk-adaptive approach that adjusts to transaction sensitivity and context. Supports multiple authentication factors with clear escalation paths. Can be combined with continuous risk assessment for dynamic policy adjustment.
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: UX complexity - unexpected authentication challenges can confuse users and cause abandonment. Requires well-defined sensitivity classification for all resources and actions. Implementation complexity in maintaining per-session authentication state and level tracking. OIDC acr_values support varies across IdPs. Timeout policies for elevated sessions need careful tuning (too short = user friction, too long = security risk). Difficult to implement for non-interactive API flows.

architecture_patterns.name: Cross-Domain Trust (AD Forest Trust / Kerberos Realm Trust)
architecture_patterns.description: Cross-Domain Trust establishes authentication and authorization trust relationships between separate Active Directory forests (or Kerberos realms), enabling users in one domain to access resources in another without separate credentials. AD forest trusts can be one-way or two-way, transitive or non-transitive, and support selective authentication (which controls which users in the trusted forest can authenticate to the trusting forest). Cross-realm Kerberos trust uses shared secrets between Key Distribution Centers (KDCs) to enable ticket-granting across realm boundaries. This pattern is fundamental in large enterprise environments with multiple AD forests (common after mergers/acquisitions), in organizations with separate production and corporate forests, and in hybrid environments bridging on-premises AD with cloud directories. The Microsoft Entra ID cross-tenant trust and AWS IAM role trust are cloud-native evolutions of this pattern.
architecture_patterns.when_to_use:
- Mergers and acquisitions where separate AD forests must share resource access
- Organizations with separate forests for different security zones (corporate vs. production)
- Cross-realm Kerberos authentication in heterogeneous Unix/Windows environments
- Hybrid environments bridging on-premises AD with Azure AD / Entra ID
- Multi-national organizations with regional AD forests needing cross-region access
architecture_patterns.when_not_to_use: Avoid two-way transitive trusts between forests with different security postures (a compromised lower-security forest becomes a path to the higher-security forest). Not suitable for B2B partner access (use SAML/OIDC federation instead of AD trust). Kerberos-based trust does not work over the internet without VPN (firewall and latency constraints). Avoid when cloud-native federation (Azure AD B2B, AWS STS AssumeRole) provides a simpler alternative.
architecture_patterns.data_flow: 1. User in Forest A requests access to a resource in Forest B. 2. Client's KDC in Forest A issues a referral ticket to Forest B's KDC. 3. Forest B's KDC validates the trust relationship and issues a service ticket for the requested resource. 4. SID filtering removes any SID history entries that could enable privilege escalation across the trust. 5. Selective authentication is evaluated: is this specific user permitted to authenticate to Forest B? 6. If authorized, the service ticket grants access; the resource server evaluates Forest B's local authorization policies. 7. Cross-forest access is logged in both forests' security event logs.
architecture_patterns.strengths: Enables seamless Windows authentication across organizational boundaries without protocol translation. Mature technology with 20+ years of enterprise deployment. Selective authentication provides granular control over which users can traverse the trust. SID filtering prevents privilege escalation through SID history manipulation. Supports Kerberos delegation across forest boundaries (constrained delegation). Well-understood by Windows administrators and security teams.
architecture_patterns.weaknesses: Kerberos-based trust requires network connectivity between forests (not internet-friendly). Two-way transitive trusts create attack paths from lower-security to higher-security forests. AD-specific technology that does not work with non-Windows identity systems. Complex troubleshooting when trust validation fails (DNS, firewall, time synchronization issues). SID history attacks (Golden Ticket, Silver Ticket) can exploit trust relationships. Being superseded by cloud-native B2B federation for new deployments. Requires VPN or ExpressRoute for cross-cloud-boundary trust.
